Em seguida, o primeiro agente analisa se a mensagem deve ser revisada. Depois disso, vai para o segundo agente, que registra o incidente no Google Sheets. Finalmente, a mensagem é excluída, o usuário é notificado e o membro do grupo é removido após três avisos. Obrigado por assistir. Deixe-nos saber nos comentários se você tiver mais perguntas.

Hello and welcome to a new Wasinger tutorial. Today we'll show you how to create a WhatsApp group moderator for your business using artificial intelligence with no code. Let's get started. However, if you've created a second API key in your Waser account, you can edit this field and add the one you prefer. Now, select the number you connected to Wasinger when creating your account. Later, we'll add a filter so it only analyzes messages coming from a group. Now, let's test the trigger to make sure everything is working correctly. Now, just for our testing process, we're going to edit the information received by the trigger so the bot can go through all the steps and we can see how it would behave in a real scenario. To do this, let's edit the incoming message and replace it with one that, for example, speaks badly about a group member. Once done, save your changes and go back to the main workflow view. Now let's create the EF node we mentioned earlier to verify in a real scenario that incoming messages come from a group and therefore should be analyzed. Click add node and type if in the search bar. Then click on it and it will be automatically added to your flow. Now let's configure it. In the value one field, add the following expression. JSON.data.hat.type. Set the condition to is equal to group. Run the test and you'll see it works correctly. [Music] Now let's rename the node to something like is group. Next we'll add our first AI agent which will analyze whether the incoming message violates the group's established rules. Inside the IF node, click add under the true branch. Search for AI agent and add it. Once it opens, change the source for prompt selector to define below and enter the instructions that best fit your needs. In our case, we've left a link in this video's description where you can access all the instructions and expressions we'll be using throughout the tutorial. [Music] If you run the step now, you'll get an error. Don't worry, that's completely normal. This happens because the AI agent requires a chat model and a memory connected to it to execute the instructions. To fix this, exit the AI agent and click add node. Then select chat model. In our case, we'll choose OpenAI, but you can use any AI model you prefer. Add your OpenAI credentials, which consist of an API key and an optional ID. You can create your API key at platform.openai.com/account/appi. We've also included the link in the video description. Once the credentials are added, proceed to add the memory node. Select simple memory and once the view expands, set the context window length to 20. This allows the agent to retain more context when analyzing interactions. It's not strictly necessary in this case, but we'll keep it that way. Next, under the session ID selector, choose define below and add the following expression. Isgroup do item.json.data.chid. [Music] Finally, we'll add a node called code, which we'll use to normalize the data extracted from our AI agent. This will make it easier to manage and reuse later on. You'll also find the code we used in the video description. Once you've copied and pasted the normalization code, go back to the main workflow view. Now let's add a second if node which will determine whether the incoming message should be reviewed by our agent and if so what severity level it has. Inside the node add the following expression json.violation detected equals true. In this case select the boolean true option. Next, we'll add our AI agent, which will analyze what type of violation the sender has committed. For this, we'll provide specific instructions that classify the message into three levels of severity based on the type of comment received in the group. These instructions are just a guideline. You can adjust them to your own use case. As always, set source for prompt to define below. Then, in the prompt, add the corresponding information. After that, add the instructions by selecting system message in the options and pasting them there. [Music] Now, we'll connect our chat model, OpenAI, and the memory node with the same configuration as before. [Music] Perfect. Now comes the most complex part of this workflow. But don't worry, we'll explain everything in detail so you won't have any trouble setting it up. From the tools branch, search for the Google Sheets integration. We'll use this to keep a record of users who have been reported as offenders or banned from any of your groups. Here's how it works. The AI agent captures the information about the new violation. It then checks your Google sheet to see if that user already has previous violations. If not, it creates a new record. If yes, it adds a new strike up to a maximum of three, after which the user would be banned from the group. So, continuing with the Google Sheet setup, we need to add our credentials, which you can find in your Google Cloud account. Click add credentials. And if you don't know how to connect Google tools to Wasn't Flows, don't worry. We've also included a tutorial video in the description that explains the process perfectly. Once you've configured Google Sheets correctly, select the spreadsheet your AI agent will use. After setting it up, click the share button in the top right corner. And under general access, choose and we anyone with the link editor. Now your agent will have full access to overwrite, update, and manage the document. Next, select the sheet page. In most cases, you'll only have one option. In the filters section, choose the column you want to use as a reference. In this case, we'll select group member, which checks the WhatsApp number of the member who committed the violation. Then, in the value field, add the expression shown on screen. [Music] Perfect. Now, rename your Google Sheets tool to keep things organized and clear. Next, add the two remaining Google Sheets tools, which will be used to create a new recorded user or update an existing one. For this case, change the operation to update row and select the same Google Sheets file again. The important thing now is to create our last AI agent which will create the appropriate message in response to the violation committed by the group member. Finally add the system message with the instructions to create the message depending on the strike level of the group member or if instead they will be permanently removed. Next we add the chat model with open AAI and the memory node for its correct functioning. Now we are going to add a wasn't node called delete message since when we detect that a message violates the group rules it is deleted at the same time the user is warned. So we will do the following. select our WhatsApp number connected to Wessenger and then add the expression that will return the ID of the message that the user sent and was reported by the WhatsApp trigger. We see that the expression appears in red. This is because the node cannot extract the information because the flow has not been executed. To verify that the expression is correct, click on test previous steps and wait for it to run until it reaches the delete message node. Perfect. Now it appears in green which means that the data has been extracted correctly and the value is valid. Very important remember to add in options revoke the message for all group participants. This will make sure that no one in the group can read the message again. Next we are going to create a node to send the message generated by our AI agent. Now we add the expression that will return the message created by the AI agent. Now rename the two nodes we just created and we are almost done with our workflow. Just a few more steps and it will be ready to work. Now add a new if node which will validate if the group member already has three strikes and therefore must be removed. This is done as follows. Since we have executed the flow, we can obtain from our second data normalization node the value that indicates if the user should be removed. using should remove and assigning it the value true. Hey everyone, welcome to Wasinger. In today's tutorial, I'm going to show you how to create a fully automated appointment booking system through WhatsApp using Flows, which uses N8N interface. This workflow is perfect for any business that handles timebased appointments. By the end of this video, you'll have an AI assistant that can handle the entire booking process from the initial conversation to WhatsApp confirmation without any manual intervention required. Let me show you how the complete system works. A user sends a message via WhatsApp, either text or voice. Our system automatically detects the message type, processes the information, collects client data, checks availability in Google calendar, books the appointment, and sends an email and WhatsApp confirmation. All of this happens in a completely natural conversation as if they were talking to a human being. Now, here's the best part. You don't have to build this entire workflow from scratch. This complete system is available as a readytouse template in Winger flows. You can register for free at Wasn't browse in the template library and select this WhatsApp appointment booking template. Then we'll just need to finish the configuration together. This saves you a lot of time of setup and ensures you get all the connections right from the start. Perfect. Now you have the complete workflow structure ready. Let me walk you through each component so you understand how everything connects and what we need to configure. Step one, configure Winger trigger. First, we need to set up the messenger trigger node. This will be our entry point for all WhatsApp messages. Select your messenger device. In events, check inbound text message to capture incoming messages. In this case, only for testing since when active, the flow will understand all kinds of messages. Wasn't credentials should be automatically configured, but it's worth checking them out. Step two, message type detection. And now we'll add a switch node that we'll call message type. This node will determine whether the message is text or audio. For voice messages, we check if data media type equals audio. For text messages, we check if data type equals text. Step three, handling voice messages. If the user sends a voice message, we need to process it as follows. First, the download node retrieves the audio file using Wasingers's API. Open the download node. Everything will be preconfigured, but you'll need to set up the header off field.
